Details.Label: What is Schema Studio?
Details.Subtitle: Enterprise Data Catalog
Details.Description: Schema Studio is a centralized metadata repository designed to bridge the gap between technical SQL definitions and business logic. It provides a searchable interface for exploring views, conformed dimensions, and data lineages across the entire organization's data landscape.

Details.Label: Core Purpose
Details.Subtitle: Self-Service Discovery
Details.Description: The system is built to allow analysts and developers to quickly identify the correct data sources for financial reporting and data modeling without needing direct access to underlying SQL Server instances.

Details.Label: Database Directory
Details.Subtitle: System Inventory
Details.Description: The directory below lists every source system currently connected to Schema Studio. You can see the technical identifier used in SQL vs. the friendly business name.

Details.Label: How to Search
Details.Subtitle: 3-Step Navigation
Details.Description:
Follow the top-down dropdown sequence: 
1. Select a Database source. 
2. Choose a Business Domain (e.g., Sales, Finance). 
3. Select the specific View you wish to inspect.

The column grid below the selection will update automatically.

Details.Label: SQL Definitions
Details.Subtitle: T-SQL Source
Details.Description: Each view is built on a T-SQL script. Click the 'SQL' button on the view header to view the full source code, including joins and business logic.

Details.Label: Column Metadata
Details.Subtitle: Extended Attributes
Details.Description: The grid displays business-friendly names by default. To see data types, precision, and physical column constraints, double-click any row in the columns table.

Title: View Details
Subtitle: Columns, semantic source, physical lineage, and SQL

Details.Label: What View Details Shows
Details.Subtitle: Exploded view definition
Details.Description:
View Details expands a selected view into the columns it projects, the source objects used in its FROM/JOIN section, and the SQL text behind the selected view.

Use this dialog when you need to answer two questions at the same time: what does this view expose, and where did each projected column come from?

Details.Label: Inherited / Semantic Source
Details.Subtitle: Where business meaning comes from
Details.Description:
Inherited, or Semantic Source, identifies the object and column that provide the business meaning for the selected view column.

This is the metadata authority for Business Name, Business Description, and related documentation when inheritance is allowed.

A semantic source may be an immediate semantic view rather than the physical table. That is intentional: the view where a lookup, role, or renamed business concept is first defined owns that meaning.

Details.Label: Physical Lineage
Details.Subtitle: Where the data value comes from
Details.Description:
Physical Lineage identifies the lowest resolved database, schema, object, and column that supply the actual data value.

This is the technical path back to the source system column. It is useful for audits, impact checks, and proving where a value originates.

Physical lineage does not by itself prove business meaning. The same physical column can mean different things when joined multiple times under different roles.

Details.Label: When They Are the Same
Details.Subtitle: Direct pass-through
Details.Description:
When Semantic Source and Physical Lineage are the same, the column is effectively passing straight through from the same source object and column that owns its meaning.

This is common for base objects and simple columns where no intermediate semantic view changed the role, business name, lookup expansion, or interpretation.

Details.Label: When They Are Different
Details.Subtitle: Meaning was defined above the physical source
Details.Description:
When Semantic Source and Physical Lineage are different, the data value physically came from one place, but its business meaning was defined somewhere else in the semantic view chain.

This is expected for lookup expansions, role-specific joins, calculated columns, renamed outputs, and composed integration views.

Example: an employee name may physically come from COEMP.Name, but a service view may define whether that name means Writer, Reviewer, Salesperson, or Ticket Employee. The semantic source carries that role-specific meaning.

Details.Label: When Inheritance Is Disabled
Details.Subtitle: Meaning is intentionally defined here
Details.Description:
If inheritance is disabled for a column, the view author is saying the upstream metadata should not overwrite this column's business meaning.

Use this when the same physical source is joined more than once under different roles, or when the projected column needs its own business name and description at this view level.

Title: Column Reconciliation
Subtitle: Review parser changes against saved column metadata

Details.Label: What This Dialog Does
Details.Subtitle: Parser result compared to saved metadata
Details.Description:
Column Reconciliation compares the current parser result for a view with the column metadata currently saved in Schema Studio.

Use it after refreshing or adding a view to decide how parser-owned structure and business metadata should be staged before saving.

Details.Label: Current Parsed Values
Details.Subtitle: Values read from the live SQL definition
Details.Description:
Current Parsed Values come from annotations found during the current parser run.

If a current parsed business value is blank, the SQL did not declare a local business name or description for that projected column. For composed or integration views, this can be expected because meaning may be inherited from a semantic source.

Details.Label: Saved Value Origin
Details.Subtitle: Declared, inherited, or local override
Details.Description:
Saved business values are labeled by origin so the comparison is not misleading.

Declared values belong to a base definition. Inherited saved values belong to a non-base view where inheritance is allowed, and are populated by semantic inheritance. Local override values belong to a non-base view where inheritance is disabled.

This uses existing view and column state; no extra database field is required.

Details.Label: Keep Saved vs Accept Parsed
Details.Subtitle: Which business values survive
Details.Description:
Keep Saved preserves the saved business name, description, and inheritance setting. If the saved values are inherited, this keeps the inherited semantic meaning.

Accept New Parsed Values replaces saved business values with values from the current parse. Use this when the SQL annotations are the intended new local meaning.

Parser-owned source mapping is refreshed from the current parse when Apply Merge is used; it is informational and is not a business-value choice.

Details.Label: Base Definition Hygiene
Details.Subtitle: How base-definition state is staged
Details.Description:
During reconciliation, a base view stages its columns as base definitions. A non-base view stages its columns as non-base definitions.

That keeps integration and composed view columns from being mistaken for the authority that declared the business meaning.

Title: Domain Object Modeler
Subtitle: Compose editable domain object surfaces from base views

Details.Label: What This Page Does
Details.Subtitle: Base views into an editable object surface
Details.Description: Domain Object Modeler composes selected managed base views from one domain into a single CREATE OR ALTER VIEW statement. The generated view becomes the editable object surface used by downstream domain object editing and projection workflows.

Details.Label: Anchor Base View
Details.Subtitle: The final FROM source
Details.Description: Choose one selected base view as the anchor. The anchor determines the final FROM clause for the composed view. Selection order does not matter; changing the anchor changes the root CTE used by the final SELECT.

Details.Label: Base View Output Names
Details.Subtitle: CTE aliases for selected base views
Details.Description: Each selected base view receives an editable output name. These names become the CTE aliases used in the generated SQL and in the join editor.

Details.Label: Join Requirements
Details.Subtitle: One join per non-anchor CTE
Details.Description: Every selected non-anchor base view must have a join type and ON clause before SQL can be generated. The page inserts those structured join rows after the anchor FROM clause.

Details.Label: Remove Comments
Details.Subtitle: Strip internal source annotations
Details.Description: Remove Comments regenerates the composed SQL using source view definitions with internal annotation comments stripped through the existing read-only view definition repository cleanup function.

Details.Label: Generate, Validate, Copy
Details.Subtitle: Work from current settings
Details.Description: Generate builds SQL from the current selected base views, anchor, aliases, join rows, and comment-stripping setting. Validate runs the generated SQL through the parser. Copy places the current generated SQL on the clipboard.

Title: Domain Object Editor
Subtitle: Project fields and paths from a composed view

Details.Label: Composed View Selection
Details.Subtitle: Database, domain, composed view
Details.Description: Choose a database and domain, then select a composed managed view. Base views are intentionally excluded because this editor shapes the editable object surface produced by the modeler.

Details.Label: Graph Editing
Details.Subtitle: Selection without re-authoring SQL
Details.Description: The selected view definition supplies static SQL text for the local parser. After loading, the page manipulates the parsed CTE graph by toggling output fields and object paths.

Details.Label: Generated Results
Details.Subtitle: Trimmed SQL outputs
Details.Description: Result SQL and Result SQL CTE generate trimmed projections from the current graph state. Validate checks the generated SQL with ScriptDom and Copy places the current result on the clipboard.

Title: Base View Creator
Subtitle: Base view generation with isolated new plumbing

Details.Label: What This Page Does
Details.Subtitle: Base view creation workflow
Details.Description:
Base View Creator builds base-view SQL from source table metadata, relationship discovery, lookup display choices, and projection settings.

Use it as the active base-view creation workflow before composing broader domain objects.

Details.Label: Relationship Plumbing
Details.Subtitle: Selection and dependency model
Details.Description: The page keeps base columns, lookup projections, join dependencies, and metadata defaults coordinated so lookup display choices can generate the required SQL joins without forcing unrelated FK projection changes.

Details.Label: Database Records
Details.Subtitle: Section: database identity and business context
Details.Description:
Use the Database tab to maintain one catalog record per source database.

Database Name is the physical SQL Server database name used by metadata imports and dependency checks.

Default Schema is used when source object metadata does not provide a schema. For most SQL Server sources this remains dbo.

Business Name and Business Description are required because they are the user-facing context shown in Schema Studio.

Developer Notes are optional technical notes for maintainers.

Details.Label: Domain Records
Details.Subtitle: Section: business grouping inside a database
Details.Description:
Use the Domains tab to group source objects into functional areas such as Service, Accounting, Parts, Payroll, or Shared.

Domain names are unique within the selected database. The page checks for duplicates case-insensitively before saving.

Deleting a domain is intentionally not available here because existing object records can still carry that domain value outside a database-enforced relationship.

Details.Label: Deleting Databases
Details.Subtitle: Section: cascade behavior
Details.Description:
Deleting a database record removes the database from Schema Studio metadata maintenance.

The database constraints also delete child domain and object records tied to that database. Use this only when the catalog record and its imported metadata should be removed together.
